claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to ask you about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**First, can you tell me:**
1. **How old is your child?** (or the ages of your children if you have more than one)
2. **What is your child's name?** (This helps me keep track if we're discussing multiple children)

The age is important because:
- Children **under 16** automatically qualify
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **20 or over** generally don't qualify

Please share these details and I'll guide you through the rest of the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm asking about my daughter Alex - she's 14 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me help you determine eligibility for your daughter Alex, who is 14 years old.

Since Alex is under 16, she falls into the automatic qualification age category. Now I need to check one important requirement for children under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Alex:**

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

This is to ensure she doesn't have full-time employment. Since she's only 14, this typically isn't an issue, but I need to confirm she's not working 24 or more hours per week in paid work.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, she doesn't work at all - she's still in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's what I expected for a 14-year-old. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in her own right?** 

Specifically, benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed in her own name (not yours)?

Again, this is typically not the case for a 14-year-old in school, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, she doesn't receive any benefits in her own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program?**

This includes apprenticeships in England, Scotland, or Wales. Since she's 14 and you mentioned she's in school, I'm assuming she's not in an apprenticeship, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, she's not in an apprenticeship - just regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me move on to questions about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now I need to ask about your residency status:**

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

To qualify for Child Benefit, you need to meet the UK residency requirements. This can be satisfied in several ways:

- You normally live in the UK, OR
- You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, OR
- You have pre-settled status and meet certain conditions (working, studying, or have sufficient resources), OR
- You're a Crown servant living abroad

Do any of these apply to you? The most common situation is simply living in the UK normally.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK - I'm currently living abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since you're living abroad, I need to ask a follow-up question:

**Are you a Crown servant (or married to/in a civil partnership with a Crown servant) working abroad?**

Crown servants include:
- UK government employees posted overseas
- Members of HM Forces serving abroad
- Other officials working for the Crown in an official capacity abroad

Or alternatively:

**Are you living in certain qualifying countries while working for an EU institution or an international organization?**

If neither of these situations applies to you, unfortunately living abroad would mean you don't meet the residency requirements for Child Benefit. 

Could you let me know if either of these special circumstances applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of those apply to me - I'm just living abroad for personal reasons.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing that information. Let me navigate to the outcome based on your situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that based on the assessment, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit** for Alex at this time.

**The reason:** You do not meet the UK residency requirements.

**Why this matters:**

To claim Child Benefit, you must normally live in the UK or meet specific special circumstances, such as:
- Having settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- Having pre-settled status and meeting certain conditions (working, studying, or having sufficient resources)
- Being a Crown servant working abroad
- Living in certain qualifying countries while working for an EU institution

Since you're living abroad for personal reasons and don't fall into any of these special categories, you don't meet the residency requirements for Child Benefit.

**What you should know:**

If your circumstances change and you return to live in the UK, you would be able to claim Child Benefit at that time, as Alex meets all the child-related requirements (she's under 16, in school, not working full-time, etc.).

For more information, you can visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/eligibility

Is there anything else you'd like to know or discuss about this decision?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, that's clear - thank you for explaining. I understand that I need to be living in the UK to be eligible.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
You're very welcome! I'm glad I could help clarify the situation for you. You're correct - residency in the UK is one of the key requirements for Child Benefit eligibility.

Just to be clear, if you do move back to the UK in the future, Alex would meet all the child-related criteria, so you'd be able to apply at that time.

Let me now record the final outcome of this assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! The assessment is now complete. I wish you all the best, and if your circumstances change in the future, don't hesitate to apply for Child Benefit once you're back in the UK. Take care!

reasonings: Alex is 14 years old and meets all child-related requirements: she is under 16, not working 24+ hours per week, not receiving benefits in her own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they are living abroad for personal reasons and are not a Crown servant or living under other qualifying special circumstances. Therefore, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Alex at this time.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is ineligible for Child Benefit for their daughter Alex due to not meeting the UK residency requirements. While Alex meets all child-related eligibility criteria, the claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and does not fall under any of the special circumstance exceptions (Crown servant, settled/pre-settled status, etc.). If the claimant returns to live in the UK, they would be able to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they are living abroad for personal reasons and are not a Crown servant or living under other qualifying special circumstances. Therefore, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Alex at this time.

While Alex meets all child-related eligibility criteria, the claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and does not fall under any of the special circumstance exceptions (Crown servant, settled/pre-settled status, etc.).
